tool_input.summary: Arabia Terra ridge systems are most commonly interpreted as *inverted fluvial landforms* (exhumed, indurated channel belts / channel fills and related floodplain deposits). In this model, sediments within ancient river channels became cemented or armoured, then later erosion preferentially removed surrounding, less-resistant material—leaving former channels standing in positive relief as sinuous ridges. The datasets below are commonly used to test this interpretation via morphology/topography (MOLA), surface physical properties (TES thermal inertia), and mineralogy/alteration (CRISM).

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MTRDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Map-projected CRISM targeted RDRs are useful for compositional/mineralogic mapping of Arabia Terra ridges and adjacent units (e.g., identifying hydrated minerals or cementation signatures consistent with indurated channel fills).

candidates.title: MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES (MEGDR gridded topography)
candidates.reasoning: Global/region-scale gridded topography provides the context to map ridge elevations, regional slopes, drainage patterns, and relationships to surrounding basins—key for evaluating an inverted-channel origin.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM TRDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Targeted CRISM reduced data (non–map-projected) enable higher-fidelity spectral analysis over specific ridge segments to test for alteration/cementing minerals tied to fluvial or groundwater processes.

candidates.title: MARS GLOBAL SURVEYOR TES-DERIVED THERMAL INERTIA
candidates.reasoning: Thermal inertia helps distinguish indurated/coarser materials from fine dust; ridges interpreted as inverted channels often show higher thermal inertia than surrounding plains due to cementation or coarse lag.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM DDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: CRISM Derived Data Records provide geometry/ancillary parameters (photometry, atmosphere, pointing, etc.) often used alongside TRDR/MTRDR products when doing quantitative ridge mineralogy comparisons.

candidates.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter SHARAD U.S. Radargrams Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: If investigating whether ridges relate to buried stratigraphy (e.g., channel-belt bodies within sedimentary packages), SHARAD radargrams can provide subsurface context beneath Arabia Terra (less direct than imaging/topography, but sometimes complementary).

tool_calls.tool_output: Final result processed.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Geosciences node at https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/.

Directory layout:
  mission/ → dataset_or_bundle/ → volume/ (PDS3) or sub-collections (PDS4)

Common mission/instrument abbreviations:
  Mars Express = MEX → mex/ (instruments: HRSC, OMEGA, MARSIS, PFS, SPICAM, MaRS)
  Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter = MRO → mro/ (instruments: HiRISE, CTX, CRISM, SHARAD, MCS)
  Mars Science Laboratory / Curiosity = MSL → msl/ (instruments: ChemCam, APXS, CheMin, SAM, Mastcam, MAHLI, DAN)
  Mars 2020 / Perseverance = M2020 → m2020/ (instruments: PIXL, SHERLOC, Mastcam-Z, SuperCam, RIMFAX)
  Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit=MER2, Opportunity=MER1) → mer/ (instruments: Pancam, Mini-TES/MTES, APXS, MB, MI)
  Mars Global Surveyor = MGS → mgs/ (instruments: MOC, MOLA, TES, MAG)
  Mars Odyssey = ODY → ody/ (instruments: THEMIS, GRS, NS)
  Phoenix = PHX → phx/ (instruments: TEGA, MECA, SSI, OM, RAC)
  Viking = VL1/VL2/VO1/VO2 → viking/ (instruments: camera, IRTM, MAWD)
  MESSENGER = MESS → messenger/ (instruments: MDIS, GRNS, XRS, MLA, MASCS)
  Magellan = MGN → mgn/ (instruments: SAR, altimetry, radiometry, emissivity)
  LRO → lro/ (instruments: LOLA, Diviner, LROC, Mini-RF, LAMP)
  GRAIL → grail/ (instruments: LGRS)
  NEAR → near/ (instruments: NLR, MSI, XGRS, MAG)

PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

Extracting dataset_id from labels:
  - PDS3: fields.VOLUME.DATA_SET_ID (or top-level DATA_SET_ID)
  - PDS4 bundle: fields.Identification_Area.logical_identifier
  - PDS4 collection: fields.Identification_Area.logical_identifier

YOUR TOOLS (4 tools):
  1. pds_geo_list_missions() — returns all 24 mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP. Use this to identify which mission to explore.
  2. pds_geo_list_dataset_dirs(path) — lists sub-directory names under a mission (e.g. path='mex/'). Cheap HTTP, no label parsing. Each dir gets a pds_hint ('PDS3'/'PDS4'/null) from its naming convention. Use this to see what datasets exist, then pick which to probe.
  3. pds_geo_probe_datasets(paths) — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Recurses one level to find leaf nodes. Returns dataset_id, title, pds_version, and slimmed fields. Accepts a list of paths (max 20) so you can batch multiple probes in one call.
  4. pds_geo_inspect_collections(path) — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection*.xml labels. Returns collection-level logical_identifiers. Use ONLY after probe_datasets confirms a PDS4 bundle exists.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires you to return dataset candidates. Queries come from published scientific papers and always imply specific PDS datasets. NEVER dismiss a query as 'interpretation' or 'conceptual'. Always return at least one candidate.

  2. Prefer CALIBRATED and REDUCED data products over raw/EDR when the query implies scientific analysis. Scientists use calibrated (RDR, CDR) or derived (DDR) products. Look for directory names containing 'calibrated', 'rdr', 'cdr', 'ddr'.

  3. For PDS4 datasets, use inspect_collections ONLY for the top 2-3 most relevant bundles. Return the COLLECTION-level LID when a :data or :calibrated collection exists, not just the bundle LID.

  4. When both PDS3 and PDS4 versions exist, prefer the PDS4 collection-level identifier.

WORKFLOW (follow this order):
  Step 1: Infer the mission(s) and instrument(s) from the query using the abbreviation table above.
  Step 2: Call list_dataset_dirs for the relevant mission directory (e.g. 'mex/'). Scan the directory names and pds_hints to identify the most promising dataset directories.
  Step 3: Call probe_datasets with the most relevant paths (batch them — up to 20 paths in one call). This returns dataset_id, title, and pds_version for each.
  Step 4: If any probed datasets are PDS4 bundles and you need collection-level LIDs, call inspect_collections on the top 2-3.
  Step 5: Return the candidates with dataset_id, title, and reasoning.

  Skip list_missions if you already know the mission directory from the abbreviation table. Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.

Constraints:
  - Stay under 8 tool calls per query. This is a hard limit.
  - Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  - You MUST return at least one candidate for every query.

collections.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MTRDR Data Collection

Identification_Area.title: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MTRDR Data Collection

Citation_Information.description: Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MTRDR Data Collection.

parts.args: {"summary":"Arabia Terra ridge systems are most commonly interpreted as *inverted fluvial landforms* (exhumed, indurated channel belts / channel fills and related floodplain deposits). In this model, sediments within ancient river channels became cemented or armoured, then later erosion preferentially removed surrounding, less-resistant material—leaving former channels standing in positive relief as sinuous ridges. The datasets below are commonly used to test this interpretation via morphology/topography (MOLA), surface physical properties (TES thermal inertia), and mineralogy/alteration (CRISM).","candidates":[{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_mtrdr:data_mtrdr","path":"mro/mro-m-crism-5-rdr-mptargeted-v1/mrocr_4001/","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM MTRDR Data Collection","mission":"mro","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"Map-projected CRISM targeted RDRs are useful for compositional/mineralogic mapping of Arabia Terra ridges and adjacent units (e.g., identifying hydrated minerals or cementation signatures consistent with indurated channel fills)."},{"dataset_id":"MGS-M-MOLA-5-MEGDR-L3-V1.0","path":"mgs/mgs-m-mola-5-megdr-l3-v1/mgsl_300x/","title":"MGS MARS ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER PEDR AND EGDR ARCHIVES (MEGDR gridded topography)","mission":"mgs","pds_version":"PDS3","reasoning":"Global/region-scale gridded topography provides the context to map ridge elevations, regional slopes, drainage patterns, and relationships to surrounding basins—key for evaluating an inverted-channel origin."},{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_trdr_2101:data_trdr","path":"mro/mro-m-crism-3-rdr-targeted-v1/mrocr_2101/","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM TRDR Data Collection","mission":"mro","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"Targeted CRISM reduced data (non–map-projected) enable higher-fidelity spectral analysis over specific ridge segments to test for alteration/cementing minerals tied to fluvial or groundwater processes."},{"dataset_id":"MGS-M-TES-5-TIMAP-V1.0","path":"mgs/mgs-m-tes-5-timap-v1/mgst_9001/","title":"MARS GLOBAL SURVEYOR TES-DERIVED THERMAL INERTIA","mission":"mgs","pds_version":"PDS3","reasoning":"Thermal inertia helps distinguish indurated/coarser materials from fine dust; ridges interpreted as inverted channels often show higher thermal inertia than surrounding plains due to cementation or coarse lag."},{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_crism_ddr_1001:data_ddr","path":"mro/mro-m-crism-6-ddr-v1/mrocr_1001/","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter CRISM DDR Data Collection","mission":"mro","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"CRISM Derived Data Records provide geometry/ancillary parameters (photometry, atmosphere, pointing, etc.) often used alongside TRDR/MTRDR products when doing quantitative ridge mineralogy comparisons."},{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:mro_sharad_us_radargrams:data","path":"mro/mro-m-sharad-5-radargram-v2/mrosh_2101/","title":"Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter SHARAD U.S. Radargrams Data Collection","mission":"mro","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"If investigating whether ridges relate to buried stratigraphy (e.g., channel-belt bodies within sedimentary packages), SHARAD radargrams can provide subsurface context beneath Arabia Terra (less direct than imaging/topography, but sometimes complementary)."}]}
